entry.title: Middleware Isn't Dead. It Grew Up.
entry.content:
The recent online conversation sparked by SAP's strategic investment in n8n has been energetic, and largely for the right reasons. The excitement is warranted. The investment is significant. But some of what's circulating online conflates a few things that are worth separating clearly — and some of the most important parts of the story are getting lost in the noise.

This post is my attempt to clear that up.

 

What the n8n partnership actually is

At SAP Sapphire 2026, SAP announced a strategic investment in n8n — the open-source workflow automation platform — as part of a multi-year commercial partnership with deep product alignment.

This is a genuine strategic move, and the reason is worth stating plainly: SAP didn't partner with n8n to acquire a tool. SAP partnered with n8n to gain access to a community. n8n brings 1.7 million active builders, 1,000+ app and system integrations, and a thriving ecosystem of workflows and templates built by people who were never traditional SAP developers. That's a different builder profile — technical generalists, citizen developers, automation engineers — and it's exactly the kind of expansion the SAP developer ecosystem has long needed. You no longer need to know ABAP or CDS views to build useful business automations.

For practitioners, what this means in practice is a visual, node-based workflow canvas embedded alongside the agent and app-building capabilities in Joule Studio, SAP's unified agent-building, app development, and automation environment within the SAP Business AI Platform. Builders who live in automation-first tools will feel immediately at home. The possibilities for connecting SAP agents to external services and building agentic workflows visually are genuinely compelling.

As we look to the future, expect SAP to continue to deepen this partnership. n8n's reach across the SAP landscape will grow, but more on that direction as it develops.

What the n8n partnership is not

Here is where some of the online commentary has gone sideways.

n8n in Joule Studio is a workflow automation and agent orchestration layer. SAP Integration Suite is an enterprise integration backbone. They solve fundamentally different problems, and treating one as a substitute for the other creates real architectural risk.

Integration Suite handles the things that have to work every time, at scale, under governance: enterprise-grade process integration, B2B EDI and trading partner management, full API lifecycle from design through monetization, hybrid and sovereign deployment through Edge Integration Cell, enterprise-grade event streaming, end-to-end error handling, SLA management, and audit trails for business-critical process flows. These are the scenarios where a visual workflow canvas is a complement, not a substitute.

The more useful frame is this: Joule Studio is where you build the agent, the app, or the workflow. Integration Suite is what lets those components reach every system, every process, and every partner across your enterprise — securely and at scale. The Joule Studio positioning is explicit about this: Integration Suite is named as one of the foundational layers that makes agents enterprise-ready. The two together are additive, and practitioners are better off for it.

The differing viewpoints in the online conversation are healthy

A range of perspectives has surfaced in these discussions — some overstating what n8n changes, some appropriately cautious about what it doesn't yet do. That kind of discourse sharpens everyone's thinking. But a few misconceptions keep appearing, and the most important one to address is the idea that n8n's arrival in Joule Studio resolves the PI/PO question.

It doesn't. Not yet, and not for most scenarios.

The PI/PO clock is real

SAP Process Integration and Process Orchestration reach end of standard maintenance in 2027, with extended maintenance available through 2030. That date is not moving.

The vast majority of PI/PO workloads are traditional integration scenarios — system-to-system message routing, B2B document exchange, ABAP proxy patterns, IDoc flows, RFC and JDBC lookups — that map directly and logically to SAP Integration Suite. These are not workflow automation scenarios. Migration tooling for these patterns exists today: the Migration Assessment tool is available on the free tier of Integration Suite and can give you a complete inventory and effort estimate of your PI/PO landscape without any commitment. The Migration Tool in Cloud Integration handles the conversion for the most common adapter patterns.

If you haven't started making a plan to migrate off PI/PO, it's not too late — but it's getting there. A large landscape with 300+ interfaces can take a year or more even with the best tooling. The time to start assessing is now, not when the maintenance window starts closing.

The good news: that migration is about to get meaningfully faster. SAP announced the PI/PO Migration Agent at Sapphire 2026, with general availability targeted for Q3 2026. This agent brings AI-driven automation to the medium and high complexity migrations that previously required the most specialist effort — the ones involving custom code, complex mappings, and logic that the existing Migration Tool couldn't handle automatically. For landscapes where migration friction has been the primary blocker, this agent compresses the execution phase significantly.

If you haven't started your assessment yet, there's no reason to wait. SAP's Migration Factory offers a free migration assessment to help you understand the scope, complexity, and effort of your PI/PO landscape before you commit to anything. Apply for your free assessment here.

The bigger picture

The n8n investment, the PI/PO Migration Agent, the MCP Gateway reaching general availability this quarter, the Joule conversational interface coming to Integration Suite in Q3 — these aren't separate announcements. They're part of a coherent direction: an integration platform that is becoming intelligent from the inside, connected to the entire enterprise, and accessible to a much broader builder community than ever before.

More tools. More flexibility. More paths to get your integration and automation landscape where it needs to be.

The middleware argument hasn't been killed. It's been answered — with options.

entry.title: Re: How to Implement SAP Business One projects successfully
entry.content:
Great breakdown on the implementation steps. Having run 20+ SMB go-lives in the Hyderabad market, I’d add one critical nuance: the go-live week is where SMBs lose the most money, and it’s almost never a technical issue. It’s a data readiness and visibility problem.

Three failure patterns I see repeatedly:

1. Master data validation is skipped under time pressure.

Teams migrate from Tally or legacy spreadsheets and the item master is “good enough” on cutover night. Three weeks later, inventory reports are useless because unit of measure conversions weren’t standardized. The fix: run a parallel inventory count in the old system AND SAP B1 for 2 weeks before go-live. Any variance >2% stops go-live until resolved.

The deeper issue here is that most teams don’t have real-time visibility into the data gaps. They’re comparing spreadsheets manually. If your team can’t see batch cost discrepancies or inventory variances in real time during the parallel run, you’re flying blind into go-live.

2. User training is treated as a checkbox.

SMB owners assume the consultant will “handle it.” What actually happens: 3 PowerPoint slides, a rushed demo, and a team that can log in but can’t process a goods receipt. The result: workarounds that corrupt data, followed by 6 months of cleanup.

Better model: identify 2–3 power users per department in week 1. Train them deeply. They become your go-live support army. Give them access to live dashboards — cashflow forecasts, inventory KPIs, supplier quality alerts — so they can validate transactions in real time instead of printing reports and cross-checking on paper.

3. No cutover rollback plan.

If something breaks on go-live night, what’s the decision tree? Who decides to rollback vs. push through? This sounds obvious but almost no SMB implementation documents this. The result: 48 hours of chaos before the inevitable rollback — which costs more than if you’d planned for it.

The Go-Live Checklist That Actually Works:

  • Parallel run confirmation — both systems running simultaneously for at least 5 business days, with real-time variance tracking (not end-of-day spreadsheet comparisons)
  • Data freeze window — no master data changes 48 hours before cutover
  • Batch cost gap analysis — run a full reconciliation of component costs, batch valuations, and inventory positions across both systems. If your batch costs don’t match within 1%, stop and fix before go-live
  • Rollback decision criteria — documented: who decides, under what conditions, with what data
  • Go-live support team — internal champion + consultant available for 72 hours straight, with access to live operational dashboards
  • First-week close check — finance runs month-end in SAP B1 within 5 days of go-live

The single best investment: A formal 3-day parallel run where the team processes every real transaction in BOTH systems simultaneously — with AI-powered batch cost gap analysis running in the background to flag discrepancies before they become post-go-live fires. Teams that skip this checklist average 3–4 weeks of disruption. Teams that use it average 3–4 days.

For pharma and manufacturing companies (especially those targeting USFDA or WHO-GMP compliance), add batch traceability validation to this checklist. Every goods movement during production should link a component batch to a production order to an FG batch. The genealogy chain has to be complete before your first regulatory audit, not during it.

What specific module are you struggling with? Happy to go deeper on any of these.

— Venkata Siva Reddy Polu, TEKROI
SAP B1 implementation for Hyderabad pharma and manufacturing SMBs

entry.title: Extending S/4HANA Standard Event Header with SalesOrderType (AUART) for Event Mesh routing
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

I am working on an event-driven architecture using SAP S/4HANA and SAP Advanced Event Mesh and need some guidance on event extensibility -- unfortunately I did not find many information.

My business requirement is the following: "I want to implement event routing based on the sales order type (AUART) and the consumer destination, so that each system receives only the statuses applicable to its scope."

To achieve this via AEM topic subscriptions (wildcards), I need the SalesOrderType / AUART field to be present in the Header / Topic, not just inside the data payload.

  1. I attempted to extend the standard event (sap.s4.beh.salesorder.v1.SalesOrder.Created.v1) using a Metadata Extension to apply the @event.context.attribute annotation. However, the underlying standard RAP BO (R_SALESORDERTP / D_SalesOrderCreated) does not have the @Metadata.allowExtensions: true annotation, which blocks this approach.
  2. I also considered creating a custom RAP Event from scratch, but I've failed so far and did not find a good tutorial to help me on this.

My Questions:

  1. What is the recommended Clean Core compliant approach to promote a payload field (SalesOrderType) into the Header?

  2. Is it recommended to use BAdIs to intercept and enrich the header dynamically before it is dispatched? Or would it be more appropriate to create a custom event using the classic BOR approach?

Any advice, insights, or documentation references would be highly appreciated!

Thank you in advance.

entry.title: Re: Receiving point not available for selection on sales...
entry.content:
Hello,

To have the receiving points available for selection in your sales document you need to set the receiving points in your customer master data under tab 'Customer: Unloading Points', scrolling down you can see a table for maintaining the receiving points.

Best regards.

Hello @helga03 

In PaPM Cloud, the way you did it would work. For OnPrem, I remembered this blog post where the reply suggested using parentheses to avoid parsing as a string. Try this one:

 

13854.COUNTERPARTY_TYPE[(COUNTERPARTY_ID=M.COUNTERPARTY_ID)]

Regards 

Leo

entry.title: COPA Top-Down Distribution (TDD) Complex Scenario - MANAGE_ALLOCATION
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

I am configuring a Complex Top-Down Distribution (TDD) cycle in SAP S/4 HANA Margin Analysis (ACDOC_PA) using the Fiori app 'Manage Allocations'. I am facing several warnings and errors and would appreciate your guidance.

BUSINESS REQUIREMENT

I need to allocate overhead costs (account 622260) sitting on Company Code 0001 proportionally based on Net Sales (CA) from multiple Company Codes defined in a group called COPA_STE. The brand dimension WW150_PA = LEGRAND is used to filter both sender and reference data. The reference base is a GL account group called CA4NET.

This is a cross-company code TDD: costs reside on CoCode 0001 but the reference base (sales) comes from multiple company codes (group COPA_STE).

TDD TEMPLATE SETUP

KUNNR (Customer) is set as category D = Distributed — this is the distribution characteristic.
WW150_PA (Brand) is set as category C = Retained and relevant for reference.
RBUKRS (Company Code) is set as category C = Retained and relevant for reference.

CYCLE CONFIGURATION

Allocation Context: ACDOC_PA (Margin Analysis)
Ledger: 0L
Allocation Type: C (TOP DOWN DISTRIBUTION)
Actual/Plan: I (Actual)
Transaction Currency: X

Rules:
- Sender Rule: 1 (Posted Amounts)
- Receiver Rule: 1 (Variable Portions)
- Var. Portion Type: TSL-0 (Actual Transaction Currency)
- Scale Neg. Factors: 1

Sender Details (3 criteria):
- RACCT = Single Value 622260
- RBUKRS = Single Value 0001
- WW150_PA = Single Value LEGRAND

Receivers (1 criterion):
- KUNNR = Interval 1 to 9999999999

Receiver Basis - Selection Criteria (3 criteria):
- RACCT = Initial (empty)
- RBUKRS = Group COPA_STE
- WW150_PA = Single Value LEGRAND

Receiver Basis - Reference Base Mapping for RACCT:
- Sender Data: Single Value 622260
- Reference Data: Group CA4NET

ISSUES ENCOUNTERED

1. Error MANAGE_ALLOC/078: Too many inputs for Single Value, Interval and Group Value.
→ Occurred when RACCT had a value in both Selection Criteria AND Reference Base Mapping simultaneously.
→ Fixed by setting RACCT to Initial in Selection Criteria and keeping the mapping only in Reference Base Mapping.

→ Result: 0 result records generated and several warnings

Thank you a lot for your help !

entry.title: Ariba Classic Invoicing
entry.content:
Hello Community,
We are a mid-sized company with a global footprint and have been using Ariba Classic Buying and Invoicing for approximately four years.
On the Buying side, our processes have largely stabilized, and the system is performing as expected. However, we continue to face persistent challenges with Invoicing, particularly given our global operations.
From our perspective:

Many of the ongoing issues, bugs, and functional gaps in Ariba Classic Invoicing remain unresolved.
Recent enhancements and fixes appear to be primarily focused on Ariba Central Invoice Management, with limited improvements for the Classic Invoicing solution.
As a result, unless we consider a significant shift in our invoicing approach or technology, we anticipate continued operational inefficiencies and recurring day-to-day issues.

We have invested considerable effort in raising Service Requests (SRs) and submitting Customer Influence requests, but progress has been limited. Invoicing has remained a consistent pain point since our initial go-live.
Looking for insights from the community:

How are other customers managing or coping with the current limitations of Ariba Classic Invoicing?
Are organizations actively transitioning to newer invoicing solutions, and if so, what has your experience been?
Any practical recommendations or workarounds that have helped improve stability or reduce operational overhead?

Appreciate any guidance or shared experiences

Thanks
Ashish

entry.title: What 20+ Years of Working with SAP Has Taught Me About Successful Transformations
entry.content:
What 20+ Years of Working with SAP Has Taught Me About Successful Transformations

Introduction

Over the past two decades, I have had the opportunity to work on SAP programs across multiple industries, including manufacturing, distribution, consumer products, and food services. During this journey, I have participated in SAP implementations, upgrades, global rollouts, business process transformations, and large-scale SAP S/4HANA migration programs.

Technology has changed significantly during this time. We have moved from traditional ERP implementations to intelligent enterprise platforms powered by real-time analytics, cloud technologies, automation, and artificial intelligence. However, despite these advancements, I have noticed that the factors that determine transformation success remain surprisingly consistent.

This article shares some of the most important lessons I have learned from working on SAP programs over the years.

Technology Alone Does Not Guarantee Success

Early in my career, I believed that selecting the right technology would automatically lead to project success. Over time, I learned that technology is only one part of the equation.

I have seen technically sound implementations struggle because business users were not prepared, process changes were not fully understood, or organizational alignment was missing.

The most successful SAP programs focus equally on people, processes, governance, and technology.

Organizations that treat SAP transformations as business transformations rather than IT projects typically achieve better outcomes.

Data Quality Matters More Than Most Organizations Expect

One recurring challenge I have observed across SAP programs is data quality.

Many organizations underestimate the effort required to cleanse, standardize, and validate data before migration activities begin.

In several transformation programs, data issues became one of the largest sources of testing delays, reconciliation challenges, and post-go-live support incidents.

My experience has taught me that data preparation should begin early and remain a continuous focus throughout the transformation lifecycle.

The quality of the migrated data often determines how quickly organizations realize value after deployment.

Governance Should Be Proactive, Not Reactive

Another lesson I have learned is that governance cannot be treated as a reporting exercise.

Many projects rely heavily on status updates and issue tracking. While these activities are important, effective governance requires early identification of risks and proactive decision-making.

The strongest transformation programs establish clear escalation paths, executive involvement, readiness monitoring, and structured decision-making processes from the beginning.

When governance becomes reactive, issues often grow larger before corrective actions are taken.

Business Engagement Is Critical

SAP transformations affect how people perform their daily work.

Because of this, business participation cannot be limited to requirements gathering and testing activities.

Successful programs consistently involve business stakeholders throughout the project lifecycle.

This includes:

  • Process design reviews

  • Data validation activities

  • Testing participation

  • Readiness assessments

  • Change management initiatives

The more engaged the business becomes, the smoother the transition tends to be after go-live.

Testing Is About Confidence, Not Just Defect Detection

Many organizations view testing primarily as a mechanism for identifying defects.

While defect identification is important, testing serves a broader purpose.

Testing builds confidence.

It helps project teams, business users, and leadership understand whether the organization is truly prepared for deployment.

When testing is approached as a business validation exercise rather than a technical checkpoint, the quality of outcomes often improves significantly.

The Importance of Executive Sponsorship

Throughout my career, I have observed a direct relationship between executive engagement and project success.

Executive sponsors help:

  • Remove organizational barriers

  • Resolve cross-functional conflicts

  • Secure resources

  • Accelerate decision-making

  • Reinforce transformation objectives

Programs with strong executive sponsorship typically maintain better momentum and experience fewer delays.

Go-Live Is Not the Finish Line

One of the biggest misconceptions in SAP programs is that success is measured by reaching go-live.

In reality, go-live represents the beginning of a new operational phase.

The true measure of success includes:

  • User adoption

  • Business process stability

  • Operational performance

  • Issue resolution effectiveness

  • Achievement of business objectives

Organizations that invest in hypercare, support readiness, and continuous improvement often realize greater long-term value from their SAP investments.

The Growing Role of AI in SAP Transformations

Recent advancements in artificial intelligence are creating new opportunities for SAP programs.

AI is increasingly being used to support:

  • Data quality monitoring

  • Predictive analytics

  • Risk identification

  • Testing optimization

  • Project governance

  • Operational insights

While AI is not a replacement for experience and judgment, it can significantly improve visibility and decision-making when applied appropriately.

I believe AI will continue to play an increasingly important role in future SAP transformation programs.

Final Thoughts

After more than 20 years of working with SAP, one lesson stands out above all others.

Successful transformations are rarely driven by technology alone.

They succeed when organizations align people, processes, data, governance, leadership, and technology toward a common objective.

Every SAP transformation presents unique challenges, but the principles of preparation, collaboration, business engagement, and continuous improvement remain constant.

As SAP technologies continue to evolve, these fundamentals will remain just as important as the platforms themselves.

From my experience, organizations that focus on these fundamentals consistently position themselves for stronger transformation outcomes and long-term business success.

entry.title: Re: Need to activate scope item 1S4
entry.content:
Hi Amit,

Activating scope item 1S4 (Automatic Market Rates Management) in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition requires the SAP Market Rates Management service running on the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP). Whether this incurs additional costs depends entirely on your data source and consumption volume:

1. When it is free of charge (Free Tier / Trial): If you plan to upload rates from your own external data source (e.g., a local central bank API), you can utilize the Bring Your Own Rates (BYOR) data option. SAP BTP offers a Free Tier plan for BYOR, which is free of charge provided you stay within the strict capacity limits for API calls. Furthermore, for non-production and testing environments, a Free Trial for 90 days is available on Cloud Foundry, which also provides test rates from the European Central Bank (ECB).

2. When it requires additional cost (Paid BTP options):

  • Refinitiv Data Option: If you require SAP to automatically fetch market data directly from the Refinitiv provider, this option always requires a paid subscription.

  • BYOR (Production Volume): If your custom data uploads exceed the Free Tier limits, you must purchase "entitlement packages" (available in CPEA / Pay-As-You-Go models) to cover the higher volume of API requests.

Official Reference Documentation for further details:

  • BYOR Option Overview
  • SAP Market Rates Management Pricing
  • SAP Market Rates Management Overview

Best regards,

Michal

entry.title: Five Mistakes Organizations Make During SAP S/4HANA Data Migration and How to Avoid Them
entry.content:
Five Mistakes Organizations Make During SAP S/4HANA Data Migration and How to Avoid Them

Introduction

Data migration is often one of the most challenging components of an SAP S/4HANA transformation. While organizations invest significant effort in system design, testing, and cutover planning, data migration frequently becomes a source of delays, quality issues, and post-go-live support challenges.

Over the years, I have participated in multiple SAP transformation programs where data migration played a critical role in overall project success. Although every program is different, I have consistently observed a few common mistakes that increase migration risk and impact business readiness.

This article shares five lessons learned from SAP transformation programs and practical approaches organizations can use to improve migration outcomes.

Mistake #1: Starting Data Cleansing Too Late

One of the most common mistakes is treating data cleansing as a technical activity that can wait until migration development begins.

In reality, data quality issues often exist long before a transformation starts.

Common challenges include:

  • Duplicate records

  • Incomplete master data

  • Inconsistent naming conventions

  • Obsolete business partners

  • Missing attributes

Organizations that begin data cleansing early typically experience smoother testing cycles and fewer migration defects.

How to Avoid It

Start data profiling and data quality assessments as early as possible. Treat data cleansing as a business responsibility supported by the project team rather than a purely technical exercise.

Mistake #2: Underestimating Business Participation

Successful data migration requires active business involvement.

Project teams often assume that technical resources can manage migration activities independently. However, business users play a critical role in validating data quality, reviewing mapping rules, and approving migrated information.

Without business participation, migration defects frequently remain unresolved until later testing phases.

How to Avoid It

Establish clear business ownership for critical data domains and involve business stakeholders throughout migration planning, validation, and testing activities.

Mistake #3: Focusing Only on Data Extraction

Many migration programs concentrate heavily on extraction and loading activities while paying less attention to transformation logic.

Migration success depends not only on moving data but also on ensuring data is structured correctly for the target SAP S/4HANA environment.

Examples include:

  • Business Partner conversion

  • Material master harmonization

  • Chart of accounts alignment

  • Organizational structure mapping

Errors in transformation logic can create significant downstream issues.

How to Avoid It

Invest time in defining transformation rules, mapping strategies, and validation procedures before migration development begins.

Mistake #4: Treating Migration Testing as a One-Time Event

Migration testing should not be limited to a single cycle.

Organizations often perform one migration test, identify issues, and assume the process is ready for production.

In practice, multiple migration rehearsals are usually required to improve quality, refine timing estimates, and validate cutover procedures.

How to Avoid It

Conduct multiple mock migrations and use each cycle to improve performance, data quality, reconciliation accuracy, and operational readiness.

Mistake #5: Measuring Success Only by Load Completion

Completing a data load does not automatically mean the migration was successful.

The real measure of success includes:

  • Data accuracy

  • Reconciliation results

  • Business validation

  • Process execution

  • Reporting accuracy

Organizations that focus only on technical completion may overlook business-critical issues.

How to Avoid It

Establish clear migration success criteria that include both technical and business validation metrics.

The Importance of Migration Governance

Strong governance is essential throughout the migration lifecycle.

Effective governance includes:

  • Data ownership

  • Issue management

  • Validation checkpoints

  • Readiness reviews

  • Executive visibility

Governance helps organizations identify risks early and maintain alignment across business and technical teams.

Looking Ahead: The Role of AI in Data Migration

Artificial intelligence is creating new opportunities to improve migration quality and efficiency.

AI can help organizations:

  • Detect data anomalies

  • Identify duplicate records

  • Predict migration risks

  • Improve reconciliation processes

  • Monitor data quality trends

While AI is not a replacement for business expertise, it can provide valuable insights that improve migration outcomes.

Final Thoughts

Data migration is much more than a technical exercise. It is a business-critical activity that directly impacts user adoption, operational stability, and transformation success.

From my experience, organizations that begin early, engage business stakeholders, establish strong governance, and continuously validate data quality are significantly more likely to achieve successful SAP S/4HANA migration outcomes.

Avoiding these common mistakes can help reduce risk, improve readiness, and increase confidence throughout the transformation journey.

entry.title: From Agent to MCP: What Building an MCP Taught Me About the Future of AI Integration
entry.content:
1. The moment that sparked the question

I've been building automations for a few years now. Not because someone asked me to — because I genuinely wanted to see if it worked.

It started with RPA. And honestly, for what it was, it worked. It solved real problems. But every time a process changed, the bot broke. Every time something shifted upstream, I was back in the designer fixing selectors and re-mapping fields. It was useful — just fragile in a way that bothered me.

Then I added LLMs to the mix. Suddenly the automation could handle variability, could read a document instead of just clicking through a form. That was exciting. But now I had two systems with completely different failure modes sitting next to each other, and I was the glue.

So I went further. I built a custom agent from scratch — a proper one, with skills, context management, decision logic. I wrote about it here. That project taught me more about how AI systems actually behave than anything I'd read. And when it worked, it really worked.

But there's always a next thing to build.

When Joule Work Desktop was announced at Sapphire, MCP was the integration standard. I didn't overthink it — if I wanted to connect to Joule, that was the path. What I didn't expect was how much building an MCP would make me question everything I thought I already understood.

The surface looks familiar: you're still giving a model tools to call, still thinking about context, still asking yourself what the model needs to know to be useful. But the architecture underneath is different in ways that matter. And those differences aren't obvious until you're in the middle of building.

This post is about what I found there.

2. Two ways to give an LLM context: Agents with Skills vs. MCP

Both approaches solve the same fundamental problem: the model doesn't know anything about your system unless you tell it.

You define what tools exist. You describe what they do. The model decides when and how to use them. That mechanic is identical in both. If you've built one, the mental model for the other isn't foreign. You're not starting from zero.

That's where the similarity ends — and where it gets interesting.

What surprised me about the agent

I'll be honest: I went into the agent build fully expecting to suffer.

Years of writing integrations the hard way had trained me well. I had my mental checklist ready: parse the API response, map the fields, pass the values explicitly between steps, handle the edge cases nobody documented. I was prepared for a week of plumbing before I could write a single line of actual logic.

Then I typed "for each result, do this" — and the agent just... did it. A proper for-each. No code. No explicit loop. I called an API and the response landed in context already mapped, already interpretable, ready to use in the next step. The model looked at the shape of the data and figured it out.

I sat there for a second genuinely unsure if it had worked or if I was about to discover a very elegant failure.

It had worked.

That moment reset a lot of assumptions I didn't know I was carrying. The plumbing I had prepared to build never needed to exist. What was left was only the part that actually required my brain: the business rules, the scoring logic, the decision criteria. The stuff that actually matters.

What's different about MCP

With the custom agent, I was still the director. The agent had skills, the skills called APIs, I defined the flow, I could see everything happening. It was my system behaving the way I told it to.

MCP is a different mental model — and it took me a moment to fully land on it.

You build a server. You expose tools. And then you let go.

The client — in this case Joule Desktop — connects to your server, discovers what you've built, and decides how to use it based on what the user asks. You don't orchestrate the flow. You don't manage the context. You define the capability and the client figures out the rest.

The first time I really internalized that, my instinct was mild panic. What do you mean I don't control the flow? But then something clicked: I wasn't building an agent anymore. I was building an interface. A set of capabilities that any MCP-compatible client could plug into — today Joule, tomorrow something else entirely. That's not losing control. That's actually a better kind of architecture.

The real difference: who's in charge

  Custom Agent (Skills) MCP

Who orchestratesThe agentThe client (Joule, Claude, etc.)
Where context livesInside the agentInside the client
Who can use the toolsOnly that agentAny MCP-compatible client
DeploymentPlatform-boundIndependent server
What you controlEverythingThe tools and their outputs

Neither is better. The agent gave me more control and visibility — which was exactly right for a workflow that needed precise, predictable steps. MCP gave me portability and decoupling — which was exactly right for something I wanted any compatible client to use without rebuilding from scratch.

The question isn't which one is superior. The question is what you're actually trying to build.

 

3. What surprised me while building

The design changed three times. And that was the real lesson.

When I start a new server, my brain goes straight to the same place it always does: define the endpoints, figure out the data model, write the code. I've done it enough times that it's almost automatic.

That instinct completely failed me here.

The first version had too many tools, each doing too little. Classic API thinking — small, focused, composable. The kind of thing that looks great in a diagram and makes you feel like a responsible adult. The model looked at my beautiful diagram and chose the wrong tool anyway. Every time. It was combining things I hadn't anticipated, skipping steps that seemed obvious, and generally behaving like it had never seen my very clean diagram. Because it hadn't. Because it doesn't work that way.

I went back in. Second version: fewer tools, each doing more. The model seemed happier. I was not. I now had tools that behaved differently depending on the input, and I couldn't clearly explain what they did even to myself. That's usually a sign something has gone wrong.

Third version — something finally clicked. And what changed wasn't the code. It was how I was thinking about the whole thing.

You're not designing endpoints for a developer to call. You're designing capabilities for a model to reason about. The name you give a tool shapes when the model reaches for it. The description isn't documentation — it's an instruction. You're writing for a different kind of reader, and once that landed for me, the design stopped fighting back.

A rule I wish someone had told me on day one: don't start building a tool until you can describe it in one sentence to a non-developer. If you can't explain it clearly, the model won't figure it out either. Write the descriptions before you write the code. Yes, before.

Then I ran into the actual hard part. And it wasn't the server.

The server was running. The tools were working. I was feeling pretty good about myself.

And then someone asked a real question and the answer came back technically correct, well-structured, and completely useless.

That's the failure mode nobody puts in the tutorial. A model without the right context doesn't throw an error — it generates a confident, beautifully formatted response that says absolutely nothing of value. It sounds like it knows what it's talking about. It doesn't. And that's scarier than a stack trace because at least a stack trace tells you something is broken.

I ended up spending more time going back to fix the knowledge layer than I'd spent building the server in the first place. Cleaning structure, rethinking what actually needed to be there versus what was just noise dressed up as content. All work that would have been faster — and less humbling — if I'd treated it as the first problem to solve instead of the thing I'd get to eventually.

The lesson, painfully earned: LLMs don't create knowledge. They surface it. Put noise in, get noise back — in a very professional tone. The knowledge layer is the foundation. The server is just the delivery mechanism. I had those two things backwards for longer than I'd like to admit.

The part I didn't expect to enjoy: vector search

I'll be honest — before this project, "embeddings" was one of those words I nodded at confidently without fully understanding.

The vector search itself went smoother than expected. Once it was running it felt almost like cheating — you send a plain question in natural language and the right content comes back ranked by actual meaning, not keyword overlap. No brittle query strings. No AND/OR logic. Just... the right stuff, most of the time.

But getting there sent me down a rabbit hole I had not budgeted for. What embeddings actually are. Why the model you use to generate them changes what comes back. Why chunking strategy — how you split and index the source content — matters way more than it sounds like it should. Why two distance metrics that seem equivalent can return completely different results.

I didn't plan for any of that. I came out of it genuinely glad I went in — and slightly annoyed that nobody had handed me a map.

4. MCP on SAP BTP: the trust boundary as a feature

There's a version of this that lives somewhere else. A different cloud provider, external APIs for embeddings, a third-party vector database. It would have worked technically.

I didn't build that version.

The decision to run the MCP on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry wasn't just about where to host something. It was about where the knowledge base lives — and what that means when you're building inside an enterprise ecosystem.

The institutional knowledge powering the server comes from SAP sources. Running on BTP meant that authentication, connectivity, and data access were all handled through mechanisms that already existed and were already trusted. No external service credentials to manage. No custom connectors to build. No explaining to anyone why sensitive knowledge was touching a third-party API.

That last one matters more than it sounds.

In enterprise settings, the conversation about data governance can easily become longer than the conversation about the actual product. Running inside the platform's trust boundary meant that conversation was mostly resolved before it started. The data doesn't leave the ecosystem. Embeddings are generated through services already inside it. The vector search runs natively. The MCP sits on Cloud Foundry. It's the same platform all the way through — and in this context, that's a feature, not a constraint.

Using the same platform as both a data store and a vector engine was the piece that made the architecture feel coherent. I wasn't stitching together the best individual tool for each layer and hoping they'd talk to each other cleanly. One platform, one trust model, one less thing to explain to anyone who asked.

The boundary wasn't something I worked around. It was something I designed with.

5. Under the hood

I want to keep this section honest and specific. Not a sales pitch for any particular stack — just the actual pieces, how they connect, and the decisions that shaped them. 

Design

The server itself

The MCP is built in Python using FastMCP — the high-level wrapper from the official MCP SDK. If you've never used it, the experience is surprisingly close to writing a FastAPI app: you define functions, decorate them with @McP.tool(), write a proper docstring, and that's your tool. The docstring isn't optional ceremony — it's what the model reads to decide when and how to call the function. Treat it like an API contract written for a reader who can't ask clarifying questions.

@mcp.tool()
def retrieve_similar_cases(query: str, category: str, top_k: int = 3) -> str:
    """
    Retrieve similar historical cases from the vector store.
    Returns expert-validated signals: category, effort, timeline, impact.
    Use these as directional hints — NOT as the final answer.
    """
    ...

The vector search

This is where the retrieval happens. The core is a HANA Cloud query using COSINE_SIMILARITY against a REAL_VECTOR column — filtered by a dimension, ordered by semantic proximity:

SELECT TOP 3 *
FROM KNOWLEDGE_TABLE
WHERE CATEGORY = ?
ORDER BY COSINE_SIMILARITY(EMBEDDING, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) DESC

Before this query runs, the incoming text gets converted into an embedding vector via SAP AI Core. That vector is what gets compared against the stored embeddings in HANA. The model that generates the embeddings matters — consistency between ingestion time and query time is non-negotiable. If you embed your knowledge base with model A and query with model B, the cosine similarities will be meaningless. Same model, both directions, always.

For batch processing, the searches run in parallel using ThreadPoolExecutor — one connection per thread, all results collected before returning. The difference in latency between sequential and parallel retrieval across a large batch is significant enough that this wasn't optional.

Two vector indexes, one query pattern

The knowledge base has two separate tables, each with its own EMBEDDING column and its own filter logic. Both are queried using the same cosine similarity pattern, just with different filters and column mappings. The retrieval function runs both searches concurrently and returns a unified result keyed by source type:

with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=len(source_types)) as pool:
    results = dict(pool.map(_search_one, source_types))

Simple, but effective. The model gets both result sets in a single tool call and uses them independently when synthesizing the response.

The transport layer on BTP

On Cloud Foundry, the server runs as a standard Python app bound to a port via the $PORT environment variable. No custom buildpack, no containers — just a Procfile, a manifest.yml, and the app itself. The MCP endpoint is exposed over HTTPS at the app's route, which is what Joule Desktop connects to. Authentication and routing are handled by BTP's built-in infrastructure.

The only thing worth calling out: stateless_http=True in the FastMCP init. MCP sessions over HTTP are stateless by design here — each request carries its own context. That's the right model for a server that multiple clients might connect to simultaneously, and it's what made the BTP deployment straightforward without needing session affinity or sticky routing.

6. Where MCP fits in the SAP AI ecosystem

I want to be careful here not to oversell something I'm still learning. But I also think there's something real happening and it's worth saying out loud.

MCP is gaining momentum inside the SAP ecosystem faster than most integration standards do. And I think the reason is simpler than it seems: it solves a problem that everyone building with AI on SAP has been quietly struggling with — how do you give a model structured access to enterprise capabilities without building a custom integration every single time?

Skills were a good answer. They worked. But they were tightly coupled to a specific agent, on a specific platform, for a specific use case. Every new scenario meant starting over.

MCP decouples that. You build the capability once. Any compatible client can use it. Today that's Joule Desktop. Tomorrow it'll be whatever comes after Joule Desktop, and the MCP you built won't care. That's the kind of architectural bet that tends to age well.

Joule Work Desktop is already in pilot. MCP is the integration path they chose. That's not a coincidence — that's a direction. And the developers who understand how to build MCPs well right now, before it's mainstream, before there are established patterns and ready-made templates for everything, are going to have a significant head start on everyone who waits until it's the obvious thing to do.

I'm not saying drop everything and rewrite your agents as MCPs. I'm saying: if you've been curious, now is a genuinely good time to build something small and see what you learn. The protocol is approachable. The tooling is improving fast. And the mental shift it forces — from thinking about functions to thinking about capabilities a model can reason about — is one of those shifts that changes how you see the whole problem space, not just this one project.

Build something. Even if it's small. Especially if it's small.

The best way to understand what MCP actually changes is to be in the middle of building with it and suddenly realize your old instincts aren't working anymore. That moment of friction is where the learning is. Don't skip it by reading about it — go find it.

entry.title: Re: Zustellung SAP
entry.content:
Regarding this issue, I'd like to confirm with you whether you have logged into the Expert Search application？ Because the email notification feature for this KBA/Note notification requires an S-user to log into the Expert Search application and use it in order to be activated. 

This issue may also you haven't accessed for this app for a long time, or at least more than 365 days. So you need reactive it manually. If you haven't access it for over 1 year, then the system will not send you email notification. So you need click it and set Expert Search application App in SAP for Me under My Home

Regarding to the detail steps about setting in Expert Search application, please kindly check KBA 2478289 - How to get notifications for favorite SAP Notes or KBAs (set up expert search filters) 

After completing the configuration, please monitor for a while if you can receive the email notification about SAP Note/KBA.

Inactive and draft rules are not expected to be validated with the rule group.

 

Regards,

Ody

entry.title: Re: What is clean core anyway?

Since I’ve built a solid understanding of the S/4HANA Public Cloud fundamentals, I feel confident working with different systems in the coming years. As long as they follow clean core principles, especially leveraging key user extensibility, I know I can quickly understand, adapt, and continuously improve these systems.

Best regards,

Kerisha

entry.title: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 03 - Declarative field control with @mandatory
entry.content:
Among the CDS annotations for declaring constraints when modelling, there are a handful that can be used to drive behaviour and restrictions in Fiori UIs, by causing the generation of appropriate annotations in the service's metadata.

One of these annotations is @mandatory, and this causes an OData annotation to be generated on the target that corresponds to the element annotated in CDS:

<Annotations Target="...">
  <Annotation Term="..." EnumMember="..." />
</Annotations>

The OData vocabulary Common contains the term FieldControl. In the <Annotation ... /> element that is generated, there's an attribute Term which points to this term, and a second attribute EnumMember which contains the fully qualified value for that term, a value which represents "mandatory".

What is that fully qualified value?

This is a question from the June Developer Challenge on CAP Knowledge. And don't forget: always submit your answer as a hash, on its own - read the Taking part section of the intro post for more info. At the end of today, this question will be updated with links to further reading on this topic.

entry.title: **Subject: Unable to connect Eclipse BW Modeling Tools / ABAP Development Tools to SAP BW/4HANA 2023
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I deployed a SAP BW/4HANA 2023 SP04 Trial appliance through SAP Cloud Appliance Library (CAL) on Microsoft Azure.

Environment

  • SAP BW/4HANA 2023 SP04 Trial
  • SAP CAL deployment on Azure
  • Eclipse with:
    • BW Modeling Tools
    • ABAP Development Tools (ADT)
  • Accessing the system from a local Windows machine

What I have verified

  1. Appliance status is Active in CAL.
  2. CAL provides a SAP GUI connection with:
    • SID: A4H
    • Instance Number: 00
    • Client: 100
  3. Network connectivity appears to be working:
    • Port 3200 is reachable.
    • Port 44300 is reachable.

Eclipse Configuration

For both BW Project and ABAP Project creation, I am using:

  • Connection Type: Custom Application Server
  • System ID: A4H
  • Application Server:
  • Instance Number: 00

Problem

Both BW Project and ABAP Project fail before authentication with the following error:

Logon to system A4H failed
(reject connection request from <local_machine>:eclipse to <server>:sapdp00)

The error occurs even before user validation.

Additional Information

  • The SAP CAL "Connect" option only downloads a .sap file for SAP GUI.
  • I do not currently have SAP GUI installed(due to license that we don't have), so testing via SAP GUI is not yet possible.
  • The error is the same regardless of the SAP user entered.

Questions

  1. What does "reject connection request ... sapdp00" typically indicate in a BW/4HANA Trial appliance?
  2. Is there any additional SAP profile parameter or dispatcher setting that must be enabled for Eclipse BW Modeling Tools / ADT access?
  3. Is connecting through the public VM IP supported for this appliance, or should Eclipse use a different hostname from the SAP system configuration?
  4. Has anyone successfully connected BW Modeling Tools to the SAP BW/4HANA 2023 Trial appliance from CAL, and if so, what connection method was used?

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: Re: Assignment date and completion date in the same report
entry.content:
Dear @lilymar : If in case you are looking for the reports from the learning administration, then you can make use of the "User Learning Plan Audit Report (CSV)" admin report.
Please note that this would give you the entire audit history of the user's learning plan, where you can see the assigned & completion date columns. To further filter you can use the excel filter options to narrow down your results.

We tried the implementing the BADI "SD_SLS_CHECK_BEFORE_SAVE" but there is no importing parameter which says the deletion is triggered.

entry.title: Re: Confirmation of combined Orders in Mill_OC
entry.content:
Hi FHBCK,

1. Confirmation status not reflected to underlying order - Partial Confirmation status does not flow back to Original Order(Child Orders), only when your Combined Order is confirmed you will see the status of the Original Orders will also update. Here the logic is as the Operations are being processed as part of Combined Order hence the Original Order Operations become a dummy operation so all the tracking, Costing etc will be taken care by Combined Order.

2. Regarding the first ten Operations query - For this there is an option of Partial Combination which can be achieved using 'Create Combined Operations' here you will have option to select the Operation of your choice. The Operations should be in a sequence and in case you are selecting Operations from the middle then you will have to first process your Operations from the Original Order which are before the starting Combined Operations. Make sure that the Routing for Operations matches your MM and Order Header configs else you will see different results upon Operation combination.

 

In both Whole and Partial Combination after your Combined Order and Original Orders are Confirmed you can now process your Original Order for activities like GR and then mark the Combined Order as TECO.

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in May
entry.content:
SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in May

May was one of those months where everything happened at once. SAPPHIRE Orlando brought a wave of announcements for SAP Business Data Cloud — platform direction, new integrations, and some big bets on enterprise AI. And while the conference was still in full swing, the product team kept shipping: two solid Datasphere releases packed with useful enhancements for data integration, modeling, and transport.

Below you'll find my top feature picks from the May releases, a summary of the key SAPPHIRE announcements, and the usual roundup of community blogs worth reading.

 

 

My top features in May

SAP Snowflake and BDC Connect for Snowflake

Two closely related announcements that mark a big step for the BDC open partner ecosystem.

SAP Snowflake is now generally available as a solution extension — Snowflake's AI/ML and advanced data engineering capabilities delivered directly through SAP. Full Snowflake Business Critical Edition, provisioned via SAP for Me, with zero-copy access to SAP data products out of the box.

BDC Connect for Snowflake adds bidirectional data sharing on AWS for customers already running Snowflake. Same enrollment approach as BDC Connect for Databricks — data products flow in both directions, business semantics and governance intact.

Run Replication Flows with Delta Load at Scheduled Times

Long-awaited. You can now schedule replication flows that use delta-enabled objects by setting Delta Load Run to "At Scheduled Time". The flow processes all available delta records and completes cleanly after each run. No more workarounds.

Bulk Assignment of Schedule Owner for Task Chains

You can now assign a schedule owner to multiple task chains at once — no more one-by-one configuration. A small change with a big impact for anyone managing larger environments with many scheduled jobs.

Analyze the Lineage Graph of a View with Runtime Metrics

The Runtime Metrics tool now lets you analyze the full lineage graph of a view, including all underlying source objects — not just the view in isolation. Much clearer visibility into where performance bottlenecks actually live.

Transport Runtime Settings for Flows and Views

Transformation flow runtime settings — HANA run modes, batch configurations, Spark application settings — can now be included in transport packages. The same applies to view persistency runtime settings in the Data Integration Monitor. One transport, consistent behavior across dev, test, and production.

Variables in Analytic Models

When using an analytic model as a source for another analytic model, you can now customize its variables — rename them, change default values, use them in restricted measures and calculations in the consuming model. A real flexibility gain for anyone building layered analytic models.

SAP Analytics Cloud Q2 2026 — Top 5 Features

A quick look at the QRC2 2026 SAC release Orla_C put together a great video walkthrough covering the top 5:

  • Asymmetric Reporting
  • Composite Versioning
  • Job Monitor Enhancements
  • Decoupled Data Panel
  • Recent & Favorites in open/save dialogs

➡️  Watch it here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0U7xc_8WN2I

 
 

 

SAPPHIRE 2026

My personal highlight at SAPPHIRE Orlando 🌴

This year I had the privilege of opening the SAP Business Data Cloud track at SAPPHIRE Orlando with the introductory session on Monday — and the response was something I didn't expect.

The session ended up as the #1 most registered session at SAPPHIRE 2026 overall, with more than 1,800 sign-ups over two sessions. And a lot of them showed up in the room. I'm still a bit speechless. Humbled, and honestly — kind of proud.

Thank you to everyone who registered, attended, or just followed along from afar. The energy in that room made it feel like more than a tech talk.

 

What's New in SAP Business Data Cloud at SAPPHIRE

SAPPHIRE 2026 brought a wave of announcements for SAP Business Data Cloud. Here's a quick summary of what was announced.

SAP Business AI Platform — SAP BTP, SAP Business Data Cloud, and SAP Business Transformation Management are converging into a single unified platform for building, contextualizing, and governing enterprise AI. At its core sits the SAP Knowledge Graph, which maps business entities, processes, and relationships across your SAP landscape.

SAP HANA Cloud natively in BDC — HANA Cloud becomes a core component of BDC, adding a unified in-memory engine for transactional, analytical, and multi-model workloads — alongside the existing SAP Databricks and SAP Snowflake integrations.

MDG and Reltio in BDC — SAP Master Data Governance is now a component of BDC, ensuring data products are verified and governance-aligned. The Reltio acquisition is complete and will be brought directly into BDC as well, adding multi-domain master data management with AI-based entity resolution and Model Context Protocol support for multi-agent workflows.

Joule Agents in the data fabric — Data products, analytical models, and planning structures can be created through natural language. SAP Analytics Cloud story generation is part of this too.

BDC Connect for Amazon Athena — Bi-directional zero-copy integration between BDC and Amazon Athena, extending the existing Snowflake and Databricks integrations. General availability planned for H2 2026.

👉I covered all of this in a dedicated recap video 

 

My favorite blogs in May

@GregBotticchio covers a genuinely useful new feature in SAP Analytics Cloud Q2 2026: the Asymmetric Reporting Layout lets you design tables where different sections follow different structures — actuals at monthly level, forecast from a cut-off date, budget at quarterly level — all in a single table, no workarounds, no duplicated objects. If you've ever built three separate tables just to answer one business question, this one's for you.
🔗 Asymmetric Reporting Layout

@yoramfriedman breaks down a structural problem that doesn't get talked about enough: the place where business context lives can't run agents, and the place that can run agents doesn't have the context. Using S/4HANA as the example, he shows how meaning — contract terms, credit history, priority rules, semantic layers — stays behind when you extract data to a generic lake. A sharp read that explains the core problem SAP Business Data Cloud is designed to solve.
🔗 The Catch-22 of Most Business AI

@Gaurish-Dessai announces something worth knowing about: a new advanced learning journey and certification for SAP Data Architects, covering data strategy for the agentic AI era, architectural frameworks like TOGAF and DAMA-DMBOK, and SAP BDC integrations with Databricks, Snowflake, Fabric, and BigQuery. If data architecture is your game, this is your next step.
🔗 New SAP Data Architecture Learning Journey & SAP Certified Data Architect Certification is now Live

@cecilysorenson covers the latest Discovery Center updates — including a dedicated SAP BDC Service Catalog with missions, roadmaps, and transparent pricing, plus an AI Maturity Calculator developed with IDC. One central place to go from initial BDC exploration to full adoption, with practical guidance for managing SAP and non-SAP data.
🔗 What's New in SAP Discovery Center: From Inspiration to Guidance

@kurzdo93 traces the evolution of enterprise data architecture from the data warehouse era through the Lakehouse to what's needed today: a knowledge core that gives AI agents the business context to actually act, not just answer. He uses SAP Business Data Cloud to show how lake storage, intelligent compute (HANA Cloud, Databricks, Snowflake), semantic data products, and Joule agents come together as a business data fabric. A well-structured read if you want to understand the architectural "why" behind BDC.
🔗 AI has outgrown legacy data architectures like SAP BW — is your stack ready to keep up?



More blogs about SAP BDC and Datasphere to check out 👇

  • From Fragmented Landscapes to Unified Insights: Business Consent in SAP BDC

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 03 - Declarative field control with @mandatory
Re: @sap/cds-rfc Plugin and XSTRING Handling
entry.content:
Please try:

if (payload.PICTURE) {
  const base64 = payload.PICTURE.includes(",")
    ? payload.PICTURE.split(",")[1]
    : payload.PICTURE;

  const base64Buffer = Buffer.from(base64, "base64");
  payload.PICTURE = base64Buffer.toString('binary');
}
payload.MATNR = "000004711";
